title: Making Them Stick
subtitle: Communication, training & attestation

hook.metaphor: Writing a great house rule and taping it inside a cupboard nobody opens changes nothing. The rule only works once people have *heard* it, *understood* it, and *agreed* to it. Getting a policy from 'approved' to 'actually shaping behaviour' is a job in itself — and it's the step most organisations quietly skip, then wonder why nobody complies.
hook.scenario: A company approves an excellent new code of conduct and posts it on an internal page. A year later, half the staff have never heard of it, and a serious breach occurs because an employee genuinely didn't know the rule. The policy was perfect and 'published'. Why did it fail?

learn: Approval is not adoption. The gap between a published policy and a *followed* one is bridged by deliberate **communication, training, and reinforcement** — the step our company skipped (posting on a page ≠ communicating).

**Communication** means actively reaching the people the policy governs, where they actually are — not a one-time email or an intranet page they'll never visit. Roll-out, reminders, and making the policy easy to find at the moment of need all matter.

**Training** turns 'I've seen it' into 'I understand and can apply it' — especially for complex or high-risk policies. People can't follow a rule they don't understand; for important policies, explanation and examples beat a wall of text.

**Attestation** is the powerful 'I have read and agree' step: asking people to formally acknowledge a policy. It both reinforces awareness and creates a record — useful evidence (the assurance thread) that people were genuinely informed. Our breaching employee 'didn't know' precisely because there was no communication or attestation.

And reinforcement is **ongoing**, not one-and-done: people forget, new staff join, and policies change. Onboarding for new joiners, periodic refreshers, and embedding rules into everyday tools and workflows are what keep a policy alive in people's behaviour rather than fading after launch day.
bridge: So at work, you can close the gap that sinks most policies by treating 'approved' as the *start*, not the finish: *have we actively communicated this, trained people on it, asked them to attest, and built in refreshers for new joiners and changes?* A perfect policy taped inside a cupboard changes nothing — making rules stick is a deliberate, ongoing communication job.

interactions.title: Perfect policy, total failure
interactions.prompt: An excellent code of conduct was posted on an intranet page; a year later half of staff never heard of it and a breach followed. Why?

options.label: The policy was bad after all.
options.feedback: The policy was fine — the *rollout* failed. Posting on a page isn't communicating, training, or attestation. Approval was treated as the finish line instead of the start.

options.label: It was 'published' but never actively communicated, trained, or attested.
options.feedback: Exactly. Approval ≠ adoption. Without active communication, training, and an 'I've read and agree' step, even a perfect policy never reaches behaviour — and people genuinely don't know the rule.

options.label: Staff are simply at fault for not browsing the intranet.
options.feedback: Expecting people to discover rules by chance on an intranet is the failure. It's the organisation's job to actively communicate and reinforce — not staff's to go hunting.

interactions.title: Making policies stick
interactions.prompt: Match each mechanism to what it achieves.

pairs.right: Actively reaching people where they work
Turning 'I've seen it' into 'I understand and can apply it'
Formal 'I've read and agree' — awareness plus a record
Teaching new joiners the rules from day one
Periodic reminders so rules don't fade

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Approval isn't adoption — a policy only sticks if you actively communicate it, train people, get them to attest 'I've read and agree', and keep reinforcing it for new joiners and changes; a perfect rule in a cupboard changes nothing.”

options: Approval isn't adoption — people must hear, understand, and acknowledge it
checkpoint.explain: A posted-but-uncommunicated policy never reaches behaviour — communication, training, and attestation bridge the gap.

options: People formally acknowledging they've read and agree to a policy
checkpoint.explain: It reinforces awareness and creates a record that people were genuinely informed.

checkpoint.q: Keeping policies alive in behaviour requires…
options: Ongoing reinforcement — onboarding, refreshers, and embedding in workflows
checkpoint.explain: People forget and staff change, so reinforcement must be continuous, not one-and-done.
